canonical_examples.analysis: Participants first performed a dull task, then were induced to tell the next participant that it was enjoyable. Those paid $20 had an adequate external justification for lying, so little attitude change was predicted. Those paid $1 lacked sufficient external justification, creating dissonance between 'I am an honest person' and 'I said a boring task was enjoyable for almost no reward.' The theory predicts reduction through internal attitude change: reinterpreting the task as more interesting. Festinger and Carlsmith found the $1 group rated the task more positively than the $20 group.
canonical_examples.problem: In Festinger and Carlsmith's forced-compliance experiment, why did people paid $1 to praise a boring task later report liking it more than people paid $20?

canonical_examples.analysis: The believers had publicly committed to the prophecy, reorganized their lives around it, and sacrificed ordinary attachments. When the flood did not occur, the disconfirming evidence produced severe dissonance: 'our prophecy failed' conflicted with 'we are chosen participants in a true revelation.' Because commitment and social support were high, many reduced dissonance not by abandoning the belief but by adding a new cognition: their faith had saved the world. Proselytizing then supplied further social validation for the revised belief.
canonical_examples.problem: In When Prophecy Fails, why did members of the apocalyptic group intensify public proselytizing after the predicted catastrophe failed to occur?

core_claims: Psychological inconsistency among cognitions produces an aversive motivational state, not merely a logical error noticed by the person.
The pressure to reduce dissonance increases with the importance of the cognitions, the proportion of dissonant to consonant cognitions, and the difficulty of undoing the commitment.
People can reduce dissonance by changing attitudes, adding consonant cognitions, trivializing the conflict, or selectively avoiding dissonance-increasing information.
Small rewards or mild threats can produce more internal attitude change than large rewards or severe threats because they leave less external justification for counterattitudinal action.
After difficult, voluntary, and irrevocable choices, people tend to increase the attractiveness of the chosen option and decrease the attractiveness of rejected alternatives.
Groups can preserve disconfirmed beliefs when members have strong prior commitment, social support, and opportunities to reinterpret failure as confirmation.
description: Cognitive Dissonance Theory, developed by Leon Festinger, explains belief and attitude change as motivated reduction of the discomfort produced when a person holds inconsistent cognitions or acts against important self-conceptions without sufficient justification.
failure_modes: Over-reading every inconsistency as dissonance: the theory applies most strongly when the cognition implicates choice, commitment, identity, or self-evaluation.
Ignoring adequate external justification: if reward, coercion, role obligation, or uncertainty fully explains the behavior, attitude change is less likely.
Retrofitting any post hoc rationalization as dissonance reduction, making the analysis unfalsifiable unless competing explanations are specified.
Assuming dissonance always produces truth-seeking; the characteristic distortion is that discomfort can make people defend false beliefs more vigorously.
Neglecting cultural and individual differences in what counts as self-relevant inconsistency or what forms of justification are socially available.

key_vocabulary.Cognition: Any belief, attitude, perception, knowledge of behavior, or value that can stand in consonant, dissonant, or irrelevant relation to another cognition.
key_vocabulary.Consonance: A relation in which one cognition follows from, supports, or psychologically fits with another.
key_vocabulary.Dissonance: A relation in which one cognition implies the opposite of another, producing motivational pressure to reduce the inconsistency.
key_vocabulary.External justification: A situational reason, such as money, threat, duty, or social pressure, that explains counterattitudinal behavior without requiring attitude change.
key_vocabulary.Forced compliance: A situation in which a person performs behavior contrary to an attitude under inducement, making the amount of justification central to later attitude change.
key_vocabulary.Free choice paradigm: An experimental design in which choosing between attractive alternatives leads people to upgrade the chosen option and downgrade the rejected one.
key_vocabulary.Selective exposure: The tendency to seek consonant information and avoid information that would increase dissonance.
key_vocabulary.Spreading of alternatives: The post-decision increase in perceived difference between chosen and rejected options.

name: Cognitive Dissonance Theory
operative_procedure: Inventory the focal cognitions: beliefs, values, self-conceptions, commitments, public statements, and observed behaviors relevant to the case.
Identify dissonant relations by asking which cognitions psychologically imply the negation of others, especially where action conflicts with identity or belief.
Estimate dissonance magnitude from importance, commitment, voluntariness, irreversibility, and the ratio of dissonant to consonant cognitions.
Check available external justification: rewards, punishments, uncertainty, role demands, or coercion that can explain the behavior without attitude change.
Predict the easiest reduction path: change an attitude, reinterpret the behavior, add consonant cognitions, trivialize the conflict, seek social validation, or avoid disconfirming evidence.
Look for paradigm signatures: insufficient justification, effort justification, post-decision spreading of alternatives, belief-disconfirmation persistence, or selective exposure.
Separate dissonance reduction from accuracy-seeking by specifying what evidence would make the person revise toward truth versus defend the threatened cognition.
Recommend interventions by reducing threat, supplying face-saving justifications, changing commitment structures, or creating small voluntary actions aligned with the desired belief.
scope_and_limits: Cognitive Dissonance Theory is strongest for explaining attitude change after voluntary counterattitudinal action, difficult commitment, public choice, failed prophecy, and self-threatening inconsistency. It is weaker when behavior is fully explained by incentives or coercion, when attitudes are low-importance, or when the case requires a normative account of what the person ought to believe rather than a psychological account of how discomfort is reduced.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Festinger's cognitive dissonance research and standard social psychology treatments, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Leon Festinger, A Theory of Cognitive Dissonance (1957)
Leon Festinger and James M. Carlsmith, 'Cognitive Consequences of Forced Compliance' (1959)
Leon Festinger, Henry W. Riecken, and Stanley Schachter, When Prophecy Fails (1956)

when_to_use: a person has acted against a stated belief, value, or identity and then appears to revise the belief rather than the behavior
a group receives clear disconfirming evidence but responds by doubling down, recruiting, or inventing a saving interpretation
a decision between close alternatives is followed by exaggerated praise of the chosen option and disparagement of rejected options
a small reward or mild threat produces unexpectedly large attitude change after counterattitudinal behavior
someone avoids, filters, or attacks information mainly because it would make prior commitments psychologically uncomfortable
